about.beautiful.p1: Lastly, although this is a matter of opinion, we believe FTCScout is beautiful. We put a lot of effort into the design of FTCScout and think that it looks sleek, modern, and is the best looking of the FTC websites.
about.fast-secure.p1: We designed FTCScout with performance, security, and accessability in mind. FTCScout pages load fast and with all data at the same time instead of popping in slowly. As a result of optimizations, FTC Scout receives exceptional Google Lighthouse scores and A+ security ratings from several auditors.
about.feature-full.p1: FTCScout provides more advanced statistics than any other FTC website on the Internet. Although other websites offer many of the features of FTCScout, we collect them all in one place. Additionally, FTCScout offers features that no other websites do.
about.feature-full.p2: The rankings page for each event provides statistics for each team for every aspect of the game in six categories: Total, Average, OPR, Min, Max, and Standard Deviation. For Freight Frenzy we tracked 191 statistics.
about.feature-full.p3: As well as viewing these statistics for each team at an event you can also view the data for the whole season using our unparalleled Season Records Page. This page provides the same data as the event rankings page but for every event of the season. In addition you can use our highly capable filtering and sorting options to find even more information. For example here you can see the rankings of X Drive's Autonomous OPRs in California for the Power Play season.
about.feature-full.p4: Finally, starting in the 2022-2023 Power Play season, FTCScout now provides a visualization of the playing field for every match. To access this feature, just click \"View Field Layout\" in the score breakdown popup of any match.
about.p1: FTCScout is a repository of results and statistics on every aspect of FTC. View all a teams events, an event's matches, rankings and awards, the best results and matches for each season, and search for teams and events.
about.p2: Started in Los Angeles, California, FTCScout is the passion project of a group of alumni from team 16321 X Drive.
about.team.p1: FTCScout was developed by alumni from 16321 X Drive, and it is FOSS (Free and Open-Source Software), so you can contribute your ideas and improvements to it on our Github.
about.title: About
about.why-intro: Although FTCScout provides similar features to other websites we believe it has several advantages:
api.graphql.intro: The FTCScout GraphQL API provides access to all of the FTCScout's data and statistics. It is used internally by the website.
api.graphql.query-middle: or you can try the playground

api.help-prefix: If you ever need help with either API, feel free to ask for assistance on our
api.intro: FTCScout has two APIs for you to choose between: a fully featured GraphQL API and a simple to use REST API.
api.rest.docs-prefix: You can view the full documentation for this API
api.rest.events.awards: Gets a list of all the awards from the event.
api.rest.events.awards-no-404-suffix: if the event does not exist.
api.rest.events.awards-returns: Returns all scalar fields of the Award GraphQL type.
api.rest.events.get: Get an event with a certain code.
api.rest.events.get-404: s if the event does not exist.
api.rest.events.get-returns: Returns all scalar fields of the Event GraphQL type except for hasMatches.
api.rest.events.matches: Get all the matches, the scores of those matches, and the teams playing in the matches.
api.rest.events.matches-404: s if the event does not exist.
api.rest.events.matches-returns: Returns all scalar fields, the scores field, and the teams field from the Match GraphQL type.
api.rest.events.search: Get a list of events. This is the correct query to get a list of all events.
api.rest.events.search-returns: Returns all scalar fields of the Event GraphQL type except for hasMatches.
api.rest.events.teams: Get all the team event participations for the event.
api.rest.events.teams-no-404-suffix: if the event does not exist.
api.rest.events.teams-returns: Returns all scalar fields and the stats field of the TeamEventParticipation GraphQL type.
api.rest.intro: The REST API is best used for simpler cases. It is not used internally, and thus it doesn't provide access to all of our data. For example, you can't perform season record queries using it. However, if you just need team data, match data, or single event statistics, it provides an easy way to access those.
api.rest.notes.400-suffix: is used for an invalid type for any variable.
api.rest.notes.graphql-type: The shown value is the equivalent GraphQL type.
api.rest.notes.season-prefix: Seasons are always given as a year number. For example
api.rest.query-details: You do not need to add any additional headers. Please do not abuse the API or we may have to add rate limiting. If you need to make large volumes of queries, use the GraphQL API. The available routes are listed below.
api.rest.query-intro: The REST API can be queried at
api.rest.team.404: s if the team does not exist.
api.rest.team.awards: Gets a list of all the teams awards. Optionally specify the season or event.
api.rest.team.awards-no-404-suffix: if the team does not exist.
api.rest.team.awards-returns: Returns all scalar fields of the Award GraphQL type.
api.rest.team.events: Can be used to get all the team event participations for the given season.
api.rest.team.events-no-404-suffix: if the team does not exist.
api.rest.team.events-returns: Returns all scalar fields and the stats field of the TeamEventParticipation GraphQL type.
api.rest.team.get: Get a team by their number.
api.rest.team.matches: Get all the matches a team has played optionally in a specific season or at a specific event.
api.rest.team.matches-404: s if the team does not exist.
api.rest.team.matches-returns: Returns all scalar fields of the TeamMatchParticipation GraphQL type.
api.rest.team.quick-stats: Get the quick stats for a team in a specific season and region. If the season is not specified defaults to the current season. If the region is not specified defaults to the whole world.
api.rest.team.quick-stats-404: s if the team does not exist or has no events in the specified season.
api.rest.team.quick-stats-returns: Returns all fields of the QuickStats GraphQL type.
api.rest.team.returns: Returns all scalar fields of the Team GraphQL type.
api.rest.team.search: Get a list of teams. This is the correct query to get a list of all teams.
api.rest.team.search-returns: Returns all scalar fields of the Team GraphQL type.

first.faq.q1.body: Nope. While it's nice to have some experienced people on a team, all you really need is curiousity and a willingness to learn. Everyone starts somewhere, and FTC is an excellent place to learn and explore.
first.faq.q1.title: Do I need technology or robotics experience?
first.faq.q2.body: Each team has a league or region comprised of teams in the local(ish) area. These can be travelled to by car, and are generally pretty easy transportation-wise. If your team qualifies, you may advance to larger tournaments which feature teams from several leagues. These are generally further than your league. If you advance from there, you can make it to the world championships! These are located in Texas (USA), and for most teams, that's a plane ride away. While most teams don't make it to the world championships, it's always good to keep the possibility in mind when joining a team.
first.faq.q2.title: How far do I need to travel to compete?
first.faq.q3.body: Any activity carries some inherent risk, but teaching proper safety etiquette can go a long way in keeping participants safe. Make sure everyone is using their tools properly and is wearing safety glasses.
first.faq.q3.title: Are robotics safe?
first.faq.q4.body: There is no \"perfect team size\", but most teams have 6-12 students participating, and all teams need at least 2 adults to teach and mentor the students. Too many students leaves people empty-handed, while too few gives too much responsiblity on each person. More information is available in the Mentor Manual, from FIRST.
first.faq.q4.title: How many people are on a team?
first.faq.q5.body: Teams typically meet once or twice a week during the game season, though this varies from team to team.
first.faq.q5.title: How often do teams meet?
first.faq.q6.body: You can find the FIRST Help Center here, or you can chat with people in the FTC (unofficial) Discord Chat here.
first.faq.q6.title: What if I have a question that is not listed here?
first.how.p1: You have 2 options - join a team, or start a team.
first.join.p1: If you want to join a team, we maintain a convenient list of teams here. FIRST has a similar webpage here. You can sort by region, and look into each one from there. Some are affiliated with schools, and therefore may not be accepting students from other schools. Others are community teams made up of participants from the local area. While we don't maintain contact information on every team, you can search the internet for that team's name and number. Many teams have social media presences or websites.
first.join.title: Joining a team:
first.p1: On occasion, we recieve emails from mildly confused parents asking how to enroll their kids in FIRST. My best guess for why this happens is that the parent and kid come across, or are invited to, an FTC event, and are told that the scores are available on FTCStats. After spending the day watching robots and following the scores on FTCStats, their kid asks to join, and they end up hitting the email button. Reasonable enough.
first.p2: Usually, we direct them to the FIRST website or tell them how to find teams in their local area to join, but I feel like it would be easier and better if we made a guide here.
first.start.p1: You can gather interested participants from your local area, and start your own team! Be aware that registration and robot parts aren't free. FIRST has a webpage listing out the expected costs associcated with starting an FTC team, located here. They also have an example budget spreadsheet here. You may be able to offset that cost with grants and sponsorships. FIRST has a webpage with information on grants and sponsorships, located here. You may also be able to encourage your kid's school to fund and maintain a robotics team.
first.start.title: Starting a team:
first.timeline.p1: Here is the timeline assocated with FTC: Source
first.title: How to get started competing in FIRST
first.what: What is FIRST and what is FTC?
first.what.p1: FIRST stands for \"For Inspiration and Recognition of Science and Technology\". It is a non-profit organization that runs robotics programs for students, all the way from Pre-K through 12th grade. This website (unaffiliated with FIRST) is dedicated specifically to tracking scores for FIRST's FTC program (FIRST Tech Challenge), which is a robotics competition for grades 7-12. It is an international robotics competition for robots 18x18in (46x46cm) or smaller. There are thousands of teams across the world, and they compete in a different game every year.
first.what.p2: If you're looking for a different program for a younger student, or for a league for larger robots, please find more information on the FIRST website. If FTC is the right program for you, read on!
home.tagline.prefix: 一种全新的方式来追踪并侦察

opr.faq.q1.body: This is what happens when a team has a really good alliance partner, but together, they end up scoring really poorly. This can sometimes be the fault of the alliance partner's robot breaking down during a match.
opr.faq.q1.title: How does negative OPR happen?
opr.faq.q2.body: Auto/Teleop/Endgame OPR is the same as normal OPR, but only for that specific period. This gives a more detailed picture of a team's strengths and weaknesses.
opr.faq.q2.title: What is Auto/Teleop/Endgame OPR?
opr.faq.q3.body: Most often on FTCStats, penalties are not calculated in OPR, which is why you sometimes see it written as \"npOPR\" or \"Total NP OPR\". We do still make available a penalty-inclusive OPR, which you can view on the Season Records page by editing the displayed Statistics.
opr.faq.q3.title: Do penalties affect OPR?
opr.history.p1: The term \"Offensive Power Rating\" (OPR) was first coined by Scott Weingart in 2006, according to Team 2834.
opr.history.p2: He described using linear algebra to calculate the average contribution of a robot to their alliance during play. While I could walk you through each step of that process, The Blue Alliance already did an excellent job on their own post explaining the math behind OPR calculations, so I won't reinvent the wheel, and will instead refer you to them.
opr.limitations.p1: It is incredibly easy to misunderstand or misinterpret OPR. If you use it as your only metric for scouting, you're not scouting. OPR is an important tool in a good scouter's toolkit, but when all you have is a hammer, everything looks like a nail. <u>Here are some reasons not to rely on it:</u>
opr.limitations.reason1.body: In seasons with substantial defensive play, OPR will end up overlooking defense as a metric. OPR tracks how well you do, but not how well you stop the other team from succeeding. Just because a potential alliance partner does not score many points does not mean that they won't help you win the match.
opr.limitations.reason1.title: It's called Offensive Power Rating for a reason
opr.limitations.reason2.body: Early on in an event, before enough matches have been played, OPR is completely useless. Before the algorithm has enough data, it will not give relevant results. Give the algorithm at least 4 matches to figure out what's going on.
opr.limitations.reason2.title: It's only as good as the data it has
opr.limitations.reason3.body: Just because two robots have high OPRs does not mean they will work well together. If both robots have their autos perform best on the same side, only one of them will be able to perform at their best. Before picking an alliance partner, make sure to watch their matches and see how they play. Better yet, go to their booth and ask them about it.
opr.limitations.reason3.title: Robot compatibility
opr.limitations.reason4.body: OPR is exactly what it says on the tin - a rating. It assumes consistent performance, and doesn't account for improvements or declines in performance over time. If a team fixes a bug in their code, or a mechanical issue, their OPR will not account for that. You still need to scout the good old-fashioned way if you want to do it right.
opr.limitations.reason4.title: Consistency
opr.limitations.subtitle: With great power comes great responsibility
opr.p1: At the top of every team page on FTCStats is a \"Quick Stats\" panel showing a team's \"Best OPR\" across several categories. But what exactly is OPR?
opr.title: About OPR
opr.what.p1: OPR boils down to one metric - How many points does a team contribute to their alliance, on average, at an event? It is important to make the distinction that OPR is not simply your average match score, divided by 2. OPR takes into account how good your alliance partners are, to figure out on average how many points you contributed to the team.
opr.what.p2: This means that in theory, you could take the OPRs of 2 teams, add them together, and get a pretty good estimation for what the resulting score would be, were they ever to alliance.
privacy.analytics.cannot: The information we store cannot be used to:
privacy.analytics.p1: We care deeply about personal privacy. Any information we receive is information you choose to send us. Some of that information is automatically included with any request you make. Your browser makes a request every time you visit a website, or open a link to a new page on a website.
privacy.analytics.p2: We do not use your information for any other purpose, and we only collect information sent to us by your browser.
privacy.analytics.use3: User counts by platform (mobile vs desktop, etc)
privacy.analytics.use4: Times of day people use FTCStats the most
privacy.ccpa: Your rights under The California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA)
privacy.ccpa.p1: To delete your personal information: We don't collect or share personal information, so there is nothing to delete. If you would like us to delete your visit counts from our records anyway, email us at privacy@ftcscout.org with your IP Address and User-Agent String.
privacy.cookies.delete: You can delete these cookies in your browser settings.
privacy.cookies.item2: Remembering if you have dismissed a banner
privacy.cookies.list-title: Our cookies are used for the following, and the following only:
privacy.cookies.p1: The only cookies we store are functional cookies. This means that they are necessary for the page to function correctly. We do not use tracking cookies, nor do any cookies we place contain any personal information.
privacy.no-personal: We don't collect or share personal information.
privacy.p1: By your continued use of the FTCScout Website, you agree to the privacy policy located on this page, including any amendments that FTCScout makes in the future.
privacy.third-parties.p1: We do not exchange your personal information with any third parties.
privacy.title: Privacy Policy
